1.synthesis: 'After this I saw another angel coming down from heaven, having great authority, and the earth was made bright with his glory. And he called out with a mighty voice, "Fallen, fallen is Babylon the great! She has become a dwelling place for demons, a haunt for every unclean spirit, a haunt for every unclean bird, a haunt for every unclean and detestable beast"' (vv.1–2). The reason: all nations have drunk her wine of sexual immorality, the kings have committed fornication with her, and the merchants have grown rich from her excessive luxury. The divine call: 'Come out of her, my people, lest you take part in her sins, lest you share in her plagues; for her sins are heaped high as heaven, and God has remembered her iniquities' (vv.4–5). The judgment: double repayment, torment in proportion to her luxury, in a single day — death, mourning, famine, fire — 'for mighty is the Lord God who has judged her' (v.8).
1.historical_context: The angel illuminating the earth with his glory echoes the divine kābôd that filled the temple in Ezekiel 43:2 and Isaiah 60:1–3. 'Fallen, fallen is Babylon the great!' quotes Isaiah 21:9 directly — the oracle of doom against historical Babylon — and the application to Rome follows the established Jewish literary practice of reading successive empires through OT doom-texts. The 'come out of her, my people' call (v.4) draws directly on Jeremiah 51:45 ('Come out of her, my people! Run for your lives!') and Isaiah 48:20 ('Leave Babylon, flee from the Babylonians!'). The boast of v.7 — 'I sit as queen; I am not a widow; I will never mourn' — directly counters Roman imperial propaganda: Roma Aeterna (Eternal Rome) appeared on coins and in poets (Virgil, Horace) who celebrated Rome's permanence. The goddess Roma was depicted enthroned and triumphant on coins throughout the empire. The 'one day' judgment (v.8) contrasts with centuries of imperial power — divine judgment is instantaneous against what appeared permanent.
1.literary_structure: The chapter unfolds in three movements: proclamation of Babylon's fall and call to exit (vv.1–8), threefold lament of Babylon's mourners (vv.9–19), and the heavenly rejoicing with the prophetic-sign of the thrown millstone (vv.20–24). The first movement (vv.1–8) provides the theological and moral rationale for everything that follows: Babylon has made the nations drunk with her immorality and enriched the merchants with her luxury, and God has remembered her iniquities. The call 'come out of her, my people' (v.4) is addressed from within the doom oracle — God's people are still within the condemned city, and the call to exit is the pastoral heart of the proclamation: judgment is coming, but the community can separate itself from what is being judged.
1.theological_application: Gill on the fall-announcement: 'Babylon is fallen, is fallen — a certain and complete fall is intended; it is expressed in the past tense, to show the certainty of it; it is repeated to show the completeness of it; not only the fall of Pagan Rome, but the utter ruin and destruction of papal Rome is meant.' Barnes on 'come out of her, my people' (v.4): 'Come out of her, my people — remove from her; do not partake of her sins, and do not be involved in her punishment. The meaning is, that people should withdraw from connection with her — from fellowship with her sins — that they might not be involved in her punishment.' Gill on God remembering iniquities (v.5): 'God has remembered her iniquities — which he had seemed to have forgotten, while she was in the height of her prosperity and power, being long-suffering and patient; but now he will remember them all, and render to her according to them.' The double repayment (v.6) applies the lex talionis at the eschatological scale: the measure of luxury and self-glorification becomes the measure of torment. For communities that watched the Roman empire's wealth and power seem inexhaustible, the announcement of its fall in 'one day' is the testimony that all human empires exist under divine judgment.
1.christology: 'For mighty is the Lord God who has judged her' (v.8) — the judgment of Babylon is an act of divine sovereignty, and the angel's proclamation is grounded in the character of the judging God. In Revelation's christological framework, the Lord God who judges is inseparable from the Lamb who was slain: the one who bore Babylon's accumulated violence in his own death (1:5: 'freed us from our sins by his blood') is also the one whose judgment Babylon now faces. The 'come out of her, my people' call is christologically significant: the community that belongs to the Lamb is called to separate its identity and allegiance from the Babylonian system before the judgment falls. The church's Exodus from Babylon mirrors Israel's Exodus from Egypt — both are acts of divine rescue from a condemned system, both are grounded in the prior redemptive act of the Lord who calls his people out.

9.synthesis: Three groups mourn in sequence. The kings: 'And the kings of the earth, who committed sexual immorality and lived in luxury with her, will weep and wail over her when they see the smoke of her burning. They will stand far off, in fear of her torment, and say, "Alas! Alas! You great city, you mighty city, Babylon! For in a single hour your judgment has come"' (vv.9–10). The merchants weep because no one buys their cargo anymore — a catalog of 28 luxury goods follows, from gold and silver to cattle and slaves, 'even human souls' (vv.11–13). The sea captains and sailors: 'And they threw dust on their heads as they wept and mourned, crying out, "Alas, alas, for the great city where all who had ships at sea grew rich by her wealth! For in a single hour she has been laid waste"' (vv.17–19).
9.historical_context: The threefold mourning scene echoes Ezekiel's extended lament over the fall of Tyre (Ezekiel 27), which describes the mourning of Tyre's trading partners at her destruction — kings of the coastlands, merchants, and sea captains — in precisely the same sequence as Revelation 18. Tyre was the ancient world's greatest commercial hub, and its destruction by Nebuchadnezzar was the paradigmatic case of a great trading city laid waste. Revelation applies this template to Rome, the first-century successor to Tyre's commercial dominance. The catalog of 28 luxury goods (vv.12–13) — from gold, silver, precious stones, and pearls down through fine linen, purple, silk, scarlet cloth, wood, bronze, iron, marble, spices, wine, oil, flour, wheat, cattle, sheep, horses, chariots to 'human souls' — is a precise inventory of Rome's import trade, reflecting the actual commodities that flowed into Rome from across the empire and beyond. The 'human souls' (psychas anthrōpōn) at the end of the list is deliberately placed last and is the most damning: the system that traded in every luxury also traded in human beings.
9.literary_structure: The three mourning-groups are structured in escalating proximity to Rome's collapse: kings stand far off in fear (v.10), merchants also stand far off (v.15), and sea captains throw dust on their heads (v.19). Each group has the same structure: identification of relationship with Babylon, the mourning cry 'Alas! Alas! You great city!', and the 'in a single hour' refrain (vv.10, 17, 19). The threefold repetition drives home the totality of the commercial collapse: the entire economic ecosystem that Rome sustained collapses with her. The 'standing far off' of the mourners is not simply grief but self-protective separation from the judgment zone — they mourn not Rome's injustice but their own commercial loss.
9.theological_application: Matthew Henry on the mourners: 'Who are the mourners, namely, those who had been bewitched by her fornication, those who had been sharers in her sensual pleasures, and those who had been gainers by her wealth and trade — the kings and the merchants and the mariners. These all agree in their lamentation; they see her fall, they lament it, and they assign the reason for it.' Gill on the merchant-catalog: 'These merchants weeping and mourning for Babylon, shows that this is not to be understood in a literal and commercial sense; for such commodities in general as are mentioned, if not traded in by Rome, yet they are by other cities; and the merchants of other cities would not be distressed at the fall of Rome, were they still able to trade elsewhere.' The 'human souls' at the end of the cargo list (v.13) is the list's theological climax: Barnes notes, 'human bodies and souls. The traffic in slaves — in human beings — seemed to the writer the most odious form of commerce.' The inclusion of human beings as trade goods indicts the entire commercial system for its dehumanization, and makes its collapse a form of justice.
9.christology: The threefold 'in a single hour' refrain (vv.10, 17, 19) stands in sharp contrast to the Lamb's eternal reign announced in 11:15 ('he shall reign forever and ever'). Babylon's power, measured in hours; the Lamb's reign, measured in eternity. The system that the mourners weep over — one built on luxury, exploitation, and the trade in human souls — is the system that the beast built to serve the dragon, and whose collapse the Lamb's victory secured. The martyrs who died for refusing to participate in this system ('they loved not their lives even unto death', 12:11) are now vindicated by the collapse of what they refused. The commercial mourning of vv.9–19 is the answer to the martyrs' cry 'how long?' (6:10): Babylon lasted precisely as long as the Lamb permitted, and no longer.

20.synthesis: 'Rejoice over her, O heaven, and you saints and apostles and prophets, for God has given judgment for you against her!' (v.20). The prophetic sign: 'Then a mighty angel took up a stone like a great millstone and threw it into the sea, and said, "So will Babylon the great city be thrown down with violence, and will be found no more. And the sound of harpists and musicians, of flute players and trumpeters, will be heard in you no more, and a craftsman of any craft will be found in you no more, and the sound of the mill will be heard in you no more, and the light of a lamp will shine in you no more, and the voice of bridegroom and bride will be heard in you no more"' (vv.21–23). The verdict: 'for your merchants were the great ones of the earth, and all nations were deceived by your sorcery. And in her was found the blood of prophets and of saints, and of all who have been slaughtered on the earth' (vv.23–24).
20.historical_context: The call to rejoice over Babylon's fall draws on Isaiah 44:23 ('burst into song, you mountains, you forests and all your trees, for the LORD has redeemed Jacob') and Jeremiah 51:48 ('then heaven and earth and all that is in them will shout for joy over Babylon'). The mighty angel throwing the millstone enacts Jeremiah 51:63–64, where Seraiah was commanded to bind a scroll containing the doom oracle to a stone and throw it into the Euphrates — the stone's sinking signifying Babylon's irreversible fall. The catalog of silences (vv.22–23) echoes Jeremiah 25:10 and Jeremiah 7:34 — the silencing of music, craftsmanship, grinding, lamplight, and the voice of bride and groom — which Jeremiah applied to Jerusalem's desolation. The climactic indictment — 'in her was found the blood of prophets and of saints, and of all who have been slaughtered on the earth' (v.24) — echoes Jesus's application of the same language to Jerusalem (Matthew 23:35–37), now applied to Rome.
20.literary_structure: The five-verse close moves from heaven's rejoicing (v.20) through the prophetic-sign action (vv.21–23) to the final verdict (vv.23–24). The call to rejoice addresses the same threefold audience as the mourners' three groups but in reverse: where the kings, merchants, and sea captains wept, the saints, apostles, and prophets are commanded to rejoice. The rejoicing is explicitly grounded in God's judgment 'for you against her' (v.20) — the eschatological vindication is not generic justice but personal: God has judged in favor of the community that Babylon slaughtered. The millstone action (v.21) then provides the prophetic-symbolic confirmation: the city that seemed as permanent as Roman concrete is thrown 'with violence' into the sea — the same sea from which the beast rose (13:1) now receives Babylon's remains.
20.theological_application: Gill on the rejoicing call (v.20): 'Rejoice over her, thou heaven — this is said by the voice from heaven, which having called upon the saints to come out of Babylon, and to take vengeance on her, now calls upon all good men to rejoice at her ruin, while others were weeping and wailing; not at that, simply considered, but as the justice of God is displayed therein.' Barnes on the millstone action (v.21): 'The idea is, that she would be cast down with violence — suddenly and with power — and that she would utterly and finally disappear, as a millstone thrown into the deep sea sinks at once, and is seen no more.' Gill on the blood of prophets (v.24): 'Such as before mentioned — in this is another reason of her destruction, besides her luxury and idolatry, namely, her shedding the blood of the saints, with which she is said to be drunk, and therefore blood is now given her to drink.' The catalog of silences (vv.22–23) is the chapter's most moving passage: everything that makes a city alive — music, craftsmanship, commerce, lamplight, the joy of wedding celebration — is silenced permanently. The city that destroyed the life of prophets loses all its own life.
20.christology: 'For God has given judgment for you against her' (v.20) — the eschatological verdict is a legal judgment, and it is pronounced 'for' the saints, apostles, and prophets. The one who sits on the great white throne (20:11) has given this judgment in their favor. In the Johannine framework, the one who judges is the one to whom the Father has given all judgment (John 5:22: 'the Father judges no one, but has given all judgment to the Son'). The rejoicing of the saints over Babylon's fall is therefore not vindictiveness but the joy of those who see the Lamb's court vindicate what the beast's court condemned. The 'blood of prophets and of saints' found in Babylon (v.24) is the blood that the martyrs' cry (6:10) asked to be avenged — and the answer to that cry is precisely the judgment that the chapter declares in v.8: 'mighty is the Lord God who has judged her.'
